interaction_phrase: IBM Quantum and IonQ researchers teamed up
occurrence_sentence: Politics makes strange bedfellows, apparently so does quantum benchmarking. In a surprising development, IBM Quantum and IonQ researchers teamed up to reveal an alternative classical simulation algorithm for an impressive error correction study conducted by a Harvard and QuEra team and published recently in [Nature](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-023-06927-3).

Politics makes strange bedfellows, apparently so does quantum benchmarking. In a surprising development, IBM Quantum and IonQ researchers teamed up to reveal an alternative classical simulation algorithm for an impressive error correction study conducted by a Harvard and QuEra team and published recently in [Nature](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-023-06927-3).

The Harvard team acknowledged that there was still work to do and predicted that studies like the IBM-IonQ study would challenge their findings, as well as point the way to new techniques and potential innovations.
The IBM Quantum-IonQ research team also reported on potential modifications to the quantum circuit that could increase the difficulty of classical simulation.
As an aside, initially this may look like a scientific bums rush on the Harvard-QuEra team's work.

In an interview with The Quantum Insider, Dolev Bluvstein, graduate student at Harvard, said that their next steps would [include working on better ways to maintain the integrity of the quantum computing process](https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/01/12/tqi-exclusive-harvard-and-quera-scientists-say-shuttling-qubits-parallelism-may-power-the-ride-to-practical-quantum-computing/).
The IBM Quantum-IonQ research team also reported on potential modifications to the quantum circuit that could increase the difficulty of classical simulation.

interaction_phrase: collaborative effort of the researchers from IBM Quantum and IonQ — and even QuEra
occurrence_sentence: However, on a closer look, the collaborative effort of the researchers from IBM Quantum and IonQ — and even QuEra — does highligh the competitive landscape of quantum computing, but also illustrates the iterative and collaborative nature of scientific progress.

He has served as a writer, editor and analyst at The Quantum Insider since its inception.
